title: Repair or replace: a decision framework
subtitle: Embodied carbon, repair cost, and where to find parts
excerpt: A new appliance carries embodied carbon from manufacturing, often equivalent to months or years of running it. Repairing and extending its life delays the manufacturing impact and keeps material out of landfill. The decision framework here uses repair cost as a fraction of replacement cost, weighted by remaining expected life.
sourceNotes: WRAP Electrical Product Material Flows report. Right to Repair EU Directive 2024/1799. Restart Project repairability data. Carbon Trust embodied carbon in household appliances.

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon dioxide emissions associated with manufacturing, transporting, and disposing of a product — as distinct from the carbon emitted during its use. For appliances, embodied carbon is typically incurred upfront when the product is made.

content.text: Manufacturing a new washing machine produces approximately 200-300 kg CO₂e in

. At the UK grid's current carbon intensity (circa 200 g CO₂e/kWh), that is the equivalent of running the machine continuously for 6-9 weeks before the operational carbon from the new machine overtakes the old one. Extending the life of an existing appliance by five years defers that manufacturing carbon impact entirely.

A widely used rule of thumb: if the repair cost exceeds 50% of the replacement cost, replacing is probably the better economic choice. Adjust for remaining expected life, a 10-year-old appliance at end of typical lifespan warrants a lower threshold than a 2-year-old one still in peak life. The rule breaks down when a new appliance is significantly more energy efficient than the old one, or when parts are no longer available.
Manufacturer's own spare parts: most major appliance brands sell parts via their website. Look up the model number (usually on a label inside the door or on the back).
Third-party parts: eSpares (UK), Ransom Spares, and 4ourhouse stock parts for most UK appliance brands.
Repair cafes and community repair events: Restart Project (therestartproject.org) runs a directory of free repair events in the UK.
iFixit: free repair guides for electronics, appliances, and devices.
Right to Repair (EU Directive, UK policy)

EU Directive 2024/1799 gives EU consumers the right to request repair from manufacturers for common household goods (washing machines, dishwashers, televisions, bicycles, clothing, and smartphones) at reasonable cost and with a parts obligation for a set number of years after manufacture. The UK government is consulting on equivalent domestic legislation, check gov.uk for the current status.
When replacing is the right call
Safety: an appliance with irreparable electrical faults, gas leaks, or structural failures should be replaced.
No parts available: without parts, repair is not possible regardless of cost.
Efficiency: a new A-rated appliance using half the electricity of an old E-rated one may justify replacement if the energy saving over 5 years covers the embodied carbon.
